title: vibration, Cause and Effect parallels karma in Eastern thought and classical phy
content:
vibration, Cause and Effect parallels karma in Eastern thought and classical physics’ determinism, Gender principle can be seen in the idea that creation in the universe involves complementary forces (like matter/energy, order/chaos). Symbolically, they advocate a worldview where mind, matter, and spirit follow the same patterns – thus learning these laws can yield mastery over oneself and environment.
• Karma (Moral Causation) (Domain: Eastern Spirituality) – A concept common to Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, etc., essentially a moral law of cause and effect: actions (intentional acts) have consequences that will return to the doer. Good intent and deeds lead to positive outcomes, while bad intent and deeds lead to suffering, either in this life or future lives. Examples: In a simple sense, helping others with pure intentions may eventually lead to others helping you or inner spiritual merits (even if not immediately visible). Malicious actions, even if unseen by others, “plant a seed” that will eventually ripen as hardship for the actor (perhaps one cheats others and in a later scenario loses everything in an unexpected way). In Buddhism, karma is not “fate” dealt by a deity, but an natural law akin to a cosmic ethical balance sheet – e.g., anger begets future anger and suffering until the cycle is broken by opposite positive action or insight. Implications: Karma provides a moral order to the universe – justice is built-in, even if delayed. It encourages personal responsibility (your current situation is influenced by past actions) and ethical living (since nothing is re
